title: Executive Order 7543—Amendment of Instructions to Diplomatic Officers and of Consular Regulations

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A 26-item omnibus internal Foreign Service administrative-housekeeping revision (exequatur procedure, naval-courtesy protocol, seamen's wage/discharge rights, shipwreck and deceased-citizen estate administration, consular fees, uniform prohibition); unusually detailed and specific throughout (e.g., a defined $5-$25 reward range for foreign rescuers, precise wage-calculation formulas for discharged seamen) rather than open discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Standing, largely mechanical procedures with specific numeric/procedural bounds.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Twenty-six distinct technical amendments are bundled into one order, but consistent with this project's treatment of EO 7449 earlier in this stream, all concern the same underlying subject (Foreign Service/consular administration) rather than substantively unrelated policy questions.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The provisions with genuine discretion (e.g., sec. 457A's private-property-acceptance judgment, sec. 328's reward-amount judgment) are each bounded by an explicit procedural safeguard or numeric range, unlike the open-ended 'unfavorable comments' language flagged in EO 7439 earlier in this stream.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing Consular Regulations and Instructions to Diplomatic Officers, revised through ordinary amendment.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Several items affirmatively add protections rather than remove them (e.g., sec. 457A's required owner waiver before accepting private property, sec. 247's anti-waiver rule protecting seamen's extra wages).

title: Executive Order 7548—Enforcement of The Convention for Safety of Life at Sea, 1929

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Confirms a detailed, mutually-negotiated jurisdictional division between the FCC and the Department of Commerce for treaty-implementation duties (radio-safety certificates vs. all other matters), directing five named agencies to exercise only functions 'they severally are now directed or authorized by law to perform' — implementing existing statutory authority for a ratified treaty, not creating new discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: The jurisdictional agreement is unusually explicit and specific for resolving inter-agency ambiguity in advance.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The one genuinely hard case (a matter affecting both vessel stability/navigability and radio certification) is given a specific, defined referral procedure (Commission must first ascertain Commerce's view; Commerce's decision is final on that sub-question) rather than being left unresolved.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing treaty-implementation arrangement; ordinary regulatory framework.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No pre-existing oversight is removed; the agreement clarifies rather than displaces each agency's existing statutory role.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: A strong exemplar of clean inter-agency design: FCC and Commerce explicitly anticipated and resolved their jurisdictional overlap in a joint, mutually-approved arrangement before it caused conflict — worth noting alongside the peer-coordination-board pattern (EO 7262/7287/7397) as a second flavor of good design, this one specific to treaty-implementation jurisdictional allocation rather than voluntary advisory coordination.

title: Executive Order 7676—The Canal Zone Judiciary

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: A detailed reorganization of the Canal Zone judiciary, implementing the Canal Zone Code with explicit cross-references to statutory authority for each provision (court divisions, town definitions, magistrate/constable appointment); the Governor's role in appointing and compensating magistrates/constables is a narrow judicial-support function anchored to specific Code sections, not open discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Clear, cross-referenced chain of responsibility (Governor, Attorney General, Marshal) for each function.

flags.name: Bundling
flags.justification: Numerous distinct administrative subjects (court boundaries, leave policy, marshal duties, magistrate/constable appointment and bonding) are bundled into one order, but consistent with this project's treatment of single-subject omnibus orders, all concern the identical Canal Zone judicial-administration structure.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The magistrate/constable compensation-setting authority is bounded to a narrow judicial-support role under an existing Code section.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Standing judicial-administration order implementing the permanent Canal Zone Code, not an emergency-tied grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Affirmatively adds accountability mechanisms rather than removing any: an explicit 120-day leave-accrual cap, a $1,000 constable bond requirement, and an explicit private right of action allowing 'any person who may be injured' to sue on a constable's bond.

flags.name: Third-Party Incentive Gaps
flags.justification: The constable-bond suit right is itself a built-in third-party remedy, reducing rather than creating an incentive gap.

flags.name: Inter-Agency Cannibalization
flags.justification: Implicates Justice (court supervision) and The Panama Canal/War (facilities); the order explicitly bars The Panama Canal from charging Justice for facilities — a clean, non-charging cooperative arrangement.

title: Executive Order 7715—Prescribing Regulations for Carrying Into Effect in the Virgin Islands Certain Provisions of the Marihuana Tax Act of 1937

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Extends a federal tax/registration compliance regime to the Virgin Islands by assigning Virgin Islands officials (Commissioner of Finance, Governor) duties explicitly modeled on established continental-U.S. analogues (Commissioner of Internal Revenue, Commissioner of Narcotics), rather than creating novel discretion.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Clear, parallel role assignments mirroring the continental structure.

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: The Governor's delegated rulemaking authority (Sec. 5) is explicitly directed to follow the existing continental-U.S. regulations 'so far as they can be made applicable,' anchoring the delegation to an established template rather than leaving it open-ended.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Implements a permanent federal tax/regulatory statute; standing regulatory framework, not an emergency grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: Establishes a new compliance regime rather than removing any pre-existing oversight; the Sec. 6 inspection/enforcement authority mirrors ordinary federal tax-enforcement inspection power.

flags.name: Exemptions Architecture
flags.justification: Sec. 3's registration/tax exemption is a clean, transparent parity clause keyed directly to whether the same person would be exempt if located in the continental United States — a flagship example of well-designed exemption architecture.

title: Executive Order 7756—Delegating to the Secretary of the Interior Certain Powers and Functions Vested in the President by the Act of February 22, 1935, Ch. 18, 49 Stat. 30, as Amended, and Authorizing the Establishment of a Petroleum Conservation Division

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: Delegates presidential powers under the 'Hot Oil Act' to the Secretary of the Interior, but the statute itself constrains the delegation: powers under section 4 are explicitly excepted, and any penal regulation issued under section 5(a) requires the President's own approval before taking effect — a self-retained check rather than an unbounded grant.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Clear designated agent, explicit statutory basis, and a retained presidential-approval requirement for punitive regulations.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Implements a standing, permanent federal statute; not an open-ended emergency grant.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No oversight is removed; the President expressly retains approval authority over any regulation carrying criminal penalties.

title: Executive Order 7757—Regulations Under the Act of February 22, 1935 as Amended by the Act of June 14, 1937

flags.name: Power Concentration
flags.justification: An unusually detailed, procedurally rigorous implementation of the Hot Oil Act's contraband-oil enforcement regime — extensive defined terms, itemized recordkeeping duties by category of regulated party, and formal notice-and-hearing procedures for licensing decisions ('Tenders'), closely bounding the delegated authority rather than leaving it open.

flags.name: Accountability Gaps
flags.justification: Extremely detailed, itemized obligations for each category of regulated person (producers, refiners, pipelines, transporting agencies, etc.).

flags.name: Vague Enforcement
flags.justification: Regulation IV allows each Tender Board, 'in cases of emergency,' to 'waive any of the formal requirements of this Order' — an undefined emergency standard for bypassing the Board's own notice-and-hearing procedures — though bounded by a proviso that 'the substance thereof' must be maintained and by a requirement of ex post public notice recorded at the Board's next hearing.

flags.name: Sunset Provisions (absence of)
flags.justification: Implements a permanent federal statute; the regulatory scheme persists as long as the underlying Act remains in force, not an open-ended emergency grant unto itself.

flags.name: Preemption of Oversight
flags.justification: No congressional, judicial, or presidential check is removed; the President's separate approval retention over penal regulations (EO 7756) continues to apply.
